coredata.dc:title: Identification of bacteria by a fluorescence sensor array based on three kinds of receptors functionalized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Laibao
Qi, Peng
Zhang, Dun
coredata.dc:description: The identification of bacteria is a crucial issue in the field of medical diagnosis, environmental monitoring and food safety. Conventional methods need long culturing times, highly skilled operators, or recognition elements specific to each kind of the bacteria. Alternatively, sensor arrays offer a cost-effective and simple approach using multiple cross-reactive receptors. Here, a facilely constructed fluorescence sensing array was developed for the identification of various bacteria. The sensor array consists of three facile synthesized carbon dots functionalized with different receptors (boronic acid, polymixin and vancomycin), which could bind to all the bacteria with different affinity instead of a specific one. A unique molecular fingerprint of each kind of bacteria was obtain from the fluorescence response pattern generated from three kinds of carbon dots. By using linear discrimination analysis (LDA) of the response pattern, the effective discrimination of six bacteria studied was demonstrated. The proposed sensor array approach may pave the way for the development of simple, rapid and effective identification of multiplex bioactive samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacterial identification
Sensor array
Carbon dots
Cross-reactive receptors
Pattern recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of functionalized CDs
Identification of bacteria in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of pattern recognition of bacteria based on three different receptors-functionalized CDs. (A) Fluorescence intensity of CDs was significantly reduced due to the binding with bacteria. (B) Fluorescence pattern generated from the different responses of the CDs toward bacteria. The fluorescent sensor array contains three different receptors-functionalized CDs, and the addition of six different bacteria.
Representative TEM images of BA-CDs (A), PM-CDs (B), and Van-CDs (C).

XPS spectra of the three kinds of CDs. XPS survey spectrum (A), High resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s region (B), and high resolution XPS spectrum of B 1 s region (C) of BA-CDs; XPS survey spectrum (D), High resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s region (E), and high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s region (F) of PM-CDs; XPS survey spectrum (G), High resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s region (H), and high resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s region (I).
Zeta potential of the three kinds of CDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of BA-CDs (A), PM-CDs (B), and Van-CDs (C) at different excitation wavelength ranging from 280 to 500 nm, and the corresponding photos of three kinds of CDs under ultraviolet light (D).

Fluorescence spectra of BA-CDs (A), PM-CDs (B), and Van-CDs (C) after incubation with E. coli (OD600 = 1.0) for different time (5 min, 30 min, 60 min, 90 min, and 120 min). Relative fluorescence intensity variety ((F0-F)/F0) of the three kinds of CDs at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and an emission wavelength of 455 nm (D).
Relative fluorescence intensity variety ((F0-F)/F0) of the three kinds of CDs towards different concentration of E.coli (OD600 = 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0).

(A) Signal patterns of the relative fluorescence intensity variety ((F0-F)/F0) of the three kinds of CDs in the presence of six different bacteria: E. coli, P. aeruginosa, D. desulfuricans, S. sciuri, L. monocytogenes and S. aureus. All values represent the average of six replicates. (B) Canonical score plot for bacterial discrimination based on the CDs- based sensor array. All six kinds of bacteria were well-separated.
Dendrogram generated by hierarchical cluster analysis of the six different bacteria.

Canonical score plot for the CDs-based fluorescent sensor array against the bacteria mixtures of D. desulfuricans and L. monocytogenes with different ratios (100 + 0, 90 + 10, 70 + 30, 50 + 50, 30 + 70, 10 + 900 + 100), respectively. In each case, a bacteria concentration of OD600 = 1.0 was used.
Summary of the jackknifed classification matrix for six kinds of bacteria.
Bacterial discrimination in tap water using LDA.

head.ce:title: Identification of bacteria by a fluorescence sensor array based on three kinds of receptors functionalized carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Open studio for marine Corrosion and Protection
Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The identification of bacteria is a crucial issue in the field of medical diagnosis, environmental monitoring and food safety. Conventional methods need long culturing times, highly skilled operators, or recognition elements specific to each kind of the bacteria. Alternatively, sensor arrays offer a cost-effective and simple approach using multiple cross-reactive receptors. Here, a facilely constructed fluorescence sensing array was developed for the identification of various bacteria. The sensor array consists of three facile synthesized carbon dots functionalized with different receptors (boronic acid, polymixin and vancomycin), which could bind to all the bacteria with different affinity instead of a specific one. A unique molecular fingerprint of each kind of bacteria was obtain from the fluorescence response pattern generated from three kinds of carbon dots. By using linear discrimination analysis (LDA) of the response pattern, the effective discrimination of six bacteria studied was demonstrated. The proposed sensor array approach may pave the way for the development of simple, rapid and effective identification of multiplex bioactive samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The identification of bacteria is a crucial issue in many important fields including medical diagnosis, environmental monitoring and food safety. The well-established methods for bacterial identification including bacteria culture and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) are effective and reliable. However, these methods require long culturing times, highly skilled operators, or expensive equipment which limiting their widespread application. Recently, the simple, rapid and specific identification of bacteria were realized by using an antibody, the highly specific recognition element. [1] Since one certain specific antibody could only bind to one kind of bacteria, the synthesis of corresponding specific antibody to every kind of bacteria is needed for the identification of the targets which costs a lot of money and time. Besides, the quality of the antibody could not be guaranteed due to the differences between different batches. Hence, there have been many attempts of the researchers to explore the new type of molecular recognition elements specific to the bacteria, including aptamer, antimicrobial peptide, bacteriophage, antibiotics and so on. To date, our group have successfully developed a series of bacterial detection methods using vancomycin [2], lysozyme [3], lectin [4] and boronic acid [5] as the recognition elements. The used recognition elements are versatile to most of the bacteria and they are non-specific to the one specific bacteria. Thus, it’s a great challenge to detect the specific bacteria when there are multiple bacteria.

To solve the problem, in recent years, array-based sensing approach, namely the “chemical nose/tongue” strategy, have been widely investigated for effective identification of multiple targets. [6–8] In this method, a representative sensor array combines multiple recognition elements/receptors which are selective to a class of targets instead of a particular one [9]. The signal pattern produced from the receptor array response to the different analysts allows the effective identification of the multiple targets including small molecules [10,11], inorganic ions [12,13], and bacteria [14,15]. To date, a variety of sensor arrays have been designed for the identification of bacteria based on fluorescent polymer-nanoparticles (NPs) [16,17], enzyme-NPs [18], fluorescence dyes [15], and quantum dots [19,20]. Though the good selectivity and identification of bacteria have been achieved using these sensor arrays, the facile construction of the sensing arrays and simplification of detection procedures are still quite challenging.

Herein, we designed a novel fluorescent sensor array for identification of bacteria based on three kinds of facilely synthesized cross-reactive receptors-functionalized carbon dots (CDs), namely boronic acid, polymixin and vancomycin functionalized CDs (BA-CDs, PM-CDs and Van-CDs). The three recognition molecules are all versatile receptors which could bind to all the bacteria with different ability. Though the individual kind of CDs could not specifically identify the bacteria directly, with the help of Mathematical statistical method ‘liner discriminant analysis (LDA)’, the simple and rapid discrimination of six kinds of bacteria could be realized by the proposed fluorescent sensor array through the fluorescence response pattern generated from the fluorescence decrease of the CDs with varying degrees due to the binding ability differences of the bacteria.

Polymyxin B sulfate, 3-aminophenylboronic acid and vancomycin hydrochloride with analytical grade were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (USA). Ammonium citrate dibasic was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent CO. Ltd (China). The water used in the research are ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm). A Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to obtain the fluorescence spectra of the CDs. The morphologies of CDs were obtained through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images using a JEM-1011 instrument. Zeta potentials of CDs were measured by dynamic light scattering using a ZEN3600 instrument.

All of the CDs (BA-CDs, Van-CDs and PM-CDs) were synthesized through a facile one-pot method according to a previously reported work with slight modifications [21]. Briefly, 30 mg of ammonium citrate dibasic was separately grind with 10 mg of 3-aminophenylboronic acid, vancomycin hydrochloride and polymyxin B sulfate by mortar pestle, and react for 4 h at 180℃. Then, 10 mL ultrapure water was used to dissolve the powder which was cooled to ambient temperature, and the large precipitates were removed by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Then the solution was dialyzed to remove the unreacted molecules with a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000) in ultrapure water for overnight. The boronic acid CDs (BA-CDs), polymyxin CDs (PM-CDs), and vancomycin CDs (Van-CDs) were obtained, and the CDs solution could be stored at 4 °C for further use.

Six kinds of bacteria: Escherichia coli (E. coli), Desulfovibrio desulfuricans (D. desulfuricans), Staphylococcus sciuri (S. sciuri), Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) used in our work were washed three times and diluted to the desired concentration (OD600 = 1) with PBS. For bacterial detection, 100 μL of the bacterial suspensions of six kinds of bacteria from the same batch were mixed with 100 μL CDs in the black 96-well plates and incubated for 60 min (10 replicates per sample). Then, the fluorescence changes of CDs were measured by a TECAN M1000pro microplate reader under the excitation wavelength at 350 nm and the emission peak at 455 nm A training matrix (3 CDs × 6 bacteria × 10 replicates) was generated using (F0-F)/F0 values and further processed with LDA and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) using SPSS (version 22.0) software. And the average linkage method was used for HCA.

The proposed fluorescent sensor array was constructed with three different receptors-functionalized CDs. The three kinds of CDs (BA-CDs, PM-CDs and Van-CDs) were all expected to exhibit certain binding ability to bacterial surface, thus could serve as sensing probes in the sensing system. BA-CDs can serve as the probe based on the binding ability of boronic acid to cis-diol molecules contained on the cell wall of bacteria. [22,5,23] PM-CDs are selected due to polymyxin’s strong affinity to lipopolysaccharide (LPS) molecules on the surface of bacteria [24–26]. Vancomycin can specifically bind to (D)-Ala-(D)-Ala peptide embedded in the peptidoglycan layer on bacterial cell walls [27,28]. In the sensing system, the functionalized CDs will aggregate on the surfaces of bacteria resulting in the significant decrease of CD’s fluorescence (Scheme 1 A). The different receptors-functionalized CDs based sensor array shows a fluorescence response pattern generated from the decrease of the fluorescence of CDs with varying degrees due to their different affinity to the bacteria. Thus, the simple and rapid discrimination of different kinds of bacteria could be realized by the proposed fluorescent sensor array (Scheme 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of functionalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized CDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 nm to 500 nm are shown in Fig. 4
A-C. And the corresponding green fluorescence emission photos of three kinds of CDs under ultraviolet light were shown in Fig. 4 D. The maximum excitation wavelength of BA-CDs, Van-CDs and PM-CDs are 340 nm, 350 nm and 350 nm, respectively. Considering the optimization of detection performance, the excitation wavelength of the three kinds of CDs was selected as 350 nm. Correspondingly, the emission peak was located at 455 nm. Compared with the previous reported work [16,18], the proposed CDs based sensor array are of significant advantages, including construction of the sensing arrays and simplification of detection procedures.

To investigate the feasibility of the proposed sensing array, the changes of fluorescence intensity of the three kinds of CDs were investigated by incubating the CDs with E. coli as a model. The decline of the fluorescence intensities of the CDs could be observed with the increase of the incubation time between the CDs) and E. coli, and the relative fluorescence intensity variety ((F0-F)/F0) changed slightly after 60 min incubation (Fig. 5 ). The decline of the fluorescence intensities of the CDs could be attributed to the formation of the bacteria- CDs conjugates, which may be due to the binding affinity between the receptors of the CDs and bacteria surface instead of the electrostatic interactions between the CDs and surface of bacteria since the charges of both CDs and bacteria were negative (Fig. 3). Meanwhile, the differences in the binding affinity between the receptors of the CDs and bacteria could account for the variety of fluorescence changes of the CDs due to the presence of the bacteria (Fig. 5D). These results indicated that the proposed three different receptors-functionalized CDs based strategy could realize the pattern recognition of bacteria.

Meanwhile, the concentrations of bacteria play an important role in the performance of identification of bacteria. To assure the optimal identification performance, therefore, the changes of fluorescence responses towards the concentration of bacteria was studied using E.coli as a model. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence responses of three CDs are close and could not separate from each other when the concentrations of E.coli are low. However, when the concentration of E.coli reaches OD600 = 1.0, the fluorescence responses of three CDs vary greatly and could separate from each other. Hence, in order to achieve the optimal identification performance, the concentration of E.coli at OD600 = 1.0 are used in all the experiment.

Furtherly, a HCA was used to explore the ability of the proposed sensing array to disclose the similarities between different kinds of bacteria. HCA classify the analytes according to the similarities in a step-by-step way based on their Euclidean distances [31]. As shown in Fig. 4, the dendrogram generated from HCA by using average linkage method clearly displayed six distinct clusters, and each cluster corresponds to one kind of bacteria. Furthermore, as shown in the dendrogram (Fig. 8 ), P. aeruginosa and E. coli, L. monocytogenes and S. aureus best match at the first level, indicating the more similarity of the members. At the second level, S. sciuri is classified into the L. monocytogenes and S. aureus group, where all the three bacteria are gram-positive bacteria. In addition, the group of E. coli and P. aeruginosa are both gram-negative bacteria,which may be attributed to the different structure of cell wall of bacteria of differnt gram states. The (D)-Ala-(D)-Ala peptide embedded in the thick peptidoglycan layer surrounding gram-positive bacteria shows strong affinity to van-CDs, while the PM-CDs display a strong affinity to lipopolysaccharide molecules present on the surface of gram-negative bacteria [32].

It is of great challenge and signification to discriminate the bacteria mixtures than pure ones since bacteria are coexist in the nature environment. To study the discrimination ability towards bacteria mixture of the proposed CDs based sensor array, D. desulfuricans and L. monocytogenes were used as the model. The bacteria mixtures of D. desulfuricans and L. monocytogenes with different ratios (100 + 0, 90 + 10, 70 + 30, 50 + 50, 30 + 70, 10 + 90, 0 + 100) were identified using the proposed CDs based sensor array. In each case, a bacteria concentration of OD600 = 1.0 was used. The canonical score plot clearly shows that the mixtures of the two strains with different ratios could be well separated without any overlapping, as shown in Fig. 9 and Table S2. Hence, the proposed CDs based sensor array presented the potential for discrimination of bacteria mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of bacteria in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the discrimination ability of the proposed CDs based sensor array in real samples, 12 unknown bacteria samples spiked in tap water with a bacteria concentration of OD600 = 1.0, which were randomly selected from the six kinds of bacteria, were tested using the proposed sensor array. As shown in Table 2 , a 91.6% accuracy of bacteria discrimination of the proposed CDs based sensor array was obtained, where one sample of S.aureus was misconceived as S. sciuri, indicating that our proposed CDs based fluorescence sensor array has good potential in practical applications.

In summary, a novel fluorescent sensor array was designed for identification of bacteria based on three kinds of facile synthesized CDs functionalized with boronic acid, polymixin and vancomycin. The CDs displayed different binding abilities to bacteria due to the variety of physicochemical nature of different bacteria surfaces. The generated fluorescence pattern allowed for the good discrimination of the six kinds of bacteria with 91.6% correct accuracy and the classification of the bacteria according to the gram status of bacteria. The proposed sensor array also show satisfactory discrimination ability in bacteria mixture and real samples. Thus, our proposed sensor array provides a new strategy for simple, rapid, and effective identification of bacteria. The proposed sensor array approach may pave the way for the development of effective identification of multiplex bioactive samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 41876101), the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST (2018QNRC001), Applied Basic Research Program of Nantong (GY12017005) and AoShan Talent Program Supported by Qingdao National Laboratory for Marine Science and Technology (No. 017ASTCP-ES02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.147.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integrating recognition elements with nanomaterials for bacteria sensing
Determination of sulphate-reducing bacteria based on vancomycin-functionalised magnetic nanoparticles using a modification-free quartz crystal microbalance
Lysozyme as a recognition element for monitoring of bacterial population
Lectin functionalized ZnO nanoarrays as a 3D nano-biointerface for bacterial detection
A simple, rapid and cost-effective colorimetric assay based on the 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid functionalized silver nanoparticles for bacteria monitoring
Detection and identification of proteins using nanoparticle-fluorescent polymer “chemical nose” sensors
Sensing of proteins in human serum using conjugates of nanoparticles and green fluorescent protein
A multichannel nanosensor for instantaneous readout of cancer drug mechanisms
Selectivity and Specificity: Pros and Cons in Sensing
Array-based detection of persistent organic pollutants via cyclodextrin promoted energy transfer
Carbon nanodots-based fluorescent turn-on sensor array for biothiols
A cross reactive sensor array to probe divalent metal ions
A molecular self-assembled colourimetric chemosensor array for simultaneous detection of metal ions in water
Point-of-Care identification of Bacteria Using protein-encapsulated gold nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor array as a versatile tool for bacterial pathogen identification and analysis
Rapid and efficient identification of Bacteria Using gold-nanoparticle–Poly(para-phenyleneethynylene) constructs
Quaternized magnetic nanoparticles-fluorescent polymer system for detection and identification of bacteria
Pathogenic detection and phenotype using magnetic nanoparticle-urease nanosensor
Array-based sensing of proteins and bacteria by using multiple luminescent nanodots as fluorescent probes
Multivalent glycosylated Cu:CdS quantum dots as a platform for rapid bacterial discrimination and detection, Sensors Actuators
S. Kumar Sahu, one step synthesis of functionalized carbon dots for the ultrasensitive detection of Escherichia coli and iron (III), Sensors Actuators
Affinity sensor using 3-aminophenylboronic acid for bacteria detection
Phenylboronic acid functionalized gold nanoparticles for highly sensitive detection of Staphylococcus aureus
Colistin-functionalised CdSe/ZnS quantum dots as fluorescent probe for the rapid detection of Escherichia coli
A lipopolysaccharide binding heteromultivalent dendrimer nanoplatform for Gram negative cell targeting
Magnetic extraction of Acinetobacter baumannii using colistin-functionalized γ-Fe2O3/Au Core/Shell composite nanoclusters
Vancomycin-conjugated polythiophene for the detection and imaging of Gram-positive bacteria
Functionalized arrays of Raman-enhancing nanoparticles for capture and culture-free analysis of bacteria in human blood
Synthesis-modification integration: one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Computational methods for the analysis of chemical sensor array data from volatile analytes
Optical sensor arrays for chemical sensing: the optoelectronic nose
